title: Banana bread
subtitle: The quick loaf cake that over-ripe bananas make better
excerpt: A 900 g banana loaf made with three very ripe bananas mashed into a simple soda-leavened batter. No yeast, no waiting. The loaf is ready in about an hour from mixing to slicing; the riper the bananas, the sweeter and more flavoured the result.
sourceNotes: Banana bread in the British tradition developed from the quick bread and loaf cake tradition documented in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, and Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845). The specific banana loaf is an American tradition that entered British baking during the interwar period, when bananas became more widely available. Pre-1928 American domestic cookery literature documents banana bread using bicarbonate of soda as the leavening, with mashed very ripe banana as the primary flavour and sweetener. The recipe here uses the standard American quick-bread ratio adapted to British ingredients: plain flour, bicarbonate of soda, butter, egg, and sugar.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 loaf (900 g / 2 lb tin, cuts to 10 slices)
recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf, freeze in a bag. Toasts from frozen perfectly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bakes and keeps well for 4 days at room temperature, improving slightly as the banana flavour develops.

recipeTools.notes: 900 g (2 lb) tin, greased and lined.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bread or loaf cake leavened with bicarbonate of soda or baking powder rather than yeast. No proving time is needed; the loaf goes straight from bowl to oven. The leavening is activated by moisture and heat, not by fermentation.

content.text: — no yeast, no waiting. The leavening is bicarbonate of soda, which reacts with the natural acids in the banana and the brown sugar to produce the rise. The riper the bananas, the more the leavening has to work with, and the sweeter and more intensely banana-flavoured the loaf. Bananas with fully black skins, which are past eating as fruit, are the best ingredient here.

The method is the standard loaf cake approach: melt the butter, mix with eggs, sugar, and mashed banana, fold in the sifted dry ingredients, bake. The main failure mode is over-mixing once the flour is in — fold just until no dry flour remains. Over-mixing develops the gluten and produces a rubbery, tunnelled loaf.

items.prepNote: very ripe — black-skinned if possible, about 300 g peeled weight

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease and line a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin. Mash the {{banana}} bananas thoroughly in a large bowl — a few small lumps are fine, but the mash should be mostly smooth. Stir in the melted {{unsalted-butter}}, {{dark-brown-sugar}}, {{eggs}} beaten eggs, and {{vanilla-extract}}.

Sift the {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} directly over the wet mixture. Add the {{walnuts}} chopped walnuts if using. Fold with a spatula in a figure-of-eight motion until no dry flour streaks remain — about 12 folds. Do not stir; fold. The batter should be thick and spoonable.

Scrape the batter into the prepared tin and smooth the top. Bake for 50–55 minutes, until the top is a deep golden-brown, a skewer through the centre comes out clean (or with only a few moist crumbs — no wet batter), and the loaf feels firm when pressed in the centre. If the top is browning before 40 minutes, lay a piece of foil loosely over the tin. Leave in the tin for 20 minutes before turning out onto a rack.
